architecture.summary: UniVLR is a unified visual latent reasoning framework for multimodal LLMs that eliminates the interleaved text-and-vision design of prior work. It renders textual chain-of-thought reasoning and auxiliary images into a shared unified visual canvas, encodes this canvas via the MLLM's vision encoder to obtain visual embeddings, and trains the model in two stages to autoregressively generate compact continuous visual latent tokens that represent both reasoning and visual evidence. At inference, the model reasons entirely through latent tokens and decodes only the final answer, avoiding explicit text CoT generation and external tool calls.

biblio.title: UniVLR: Unifying Text and Vision in Visual Latent Reasoning for Multimodal LLMs

baseline.name: interleaved visual latent reasoning methods (Monet, LVR, SkiLa)

claims.statement: UniVLR improves reasoning accuracy by 5.4% while reducing the number of generated tokens by 15.2 on average
UniVLR outperforms interleaved visual latent reasoning methods that preserve the text channel, including Monet, LVR, and SkiLa, even after removing explicit text CoT during inference
UniVLR induces denser and more focused attention to auxiliary images than the interleaved design, with key regions being more consistently attended

baseline.name: explicit text tokens and image tokens

claims.statement: The hidden-state distributions of UniVLR's latent tokens are more coherent than text and image tokens, suggesting that textual reasoning and auxiliary images are better aligned within a shared visual latent space
Consistent gains across different base MLLMs demonstrate the robustness and generality of UniVLR

UniVLR removes the explicit reasoning text channel at inference time and performs reasoning through a compact sequence of unified visual latent tokens, avoiding both external tool calls and verbose text reasoning
